era_context: Sargon II's reign continues; the Neo-Assyrian Empire is consolidating its control over the Levant and Mesopotamia. In Greece, the Archaic period is developing; city-states are consolidating and beginning to establish colonies. Egypt remains fragmented under the Third Intermediate Period. China's Spring and Autumn period continues with feudal fragmentation.

events.title: Sargon II's construction of Dur-Sharrukin (Sargonsburg) and consolidation of imperial administration
events.description: Sargon II began the construction of a new capital city, Dur-Sharrukin ('Sargon's Fortress'), near Nineveh. The city was designed as a showcase of Assyrian power and served as the administrative center of the empire. The construction of Dur-Sharrukin was part of Sargon II's broader strategy to consolidate imperial authority and demonstrate Assyrian cultural and architectural achievement. The city was built over several years, with construction continuing until Sargon II's death in 705 BCE. The palace and city walls were decorated with elaborate reliefs depicting royal hunts, military campaigns, and religious ceremonies.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations of Dur-Sharrukin (conducted by French archaeologists in the 19th century)

events.certainty_note: The construction of Dur-Sharrukin is well-attested in Sargon II's inscriptions and confirmed by archaeological excavations. The city was built over several years (c. 717–707 BCE), with construction likely beginning around 717 BCE and continuing through 710 BCE and beyond.
cross_references: Part of Sargon II's reign (722–705 BCE)

events.title: Possible dating of early Etruscan settlements in Italy
events.description: The Etruscan civilization emerged in central Italy during the 8th–7th centuries BCE. Early Etruscan settlements and cultural practices are attested in the archaeological record, but the precise dating of the emergence of Etruscan civilization is debated. Some scholars place the beginning of the Etruscan period around 700 BCE, while others suggest it began earlier in the 8th century BCE. The Etruscans developed a sophisticated urban civilization with distinctive religious practices, art, and writing systems.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Etruscan sites (tombs, settlements, artifacts)
Later Greek and Roman accounts of the Etruscans

events.certainty_note: The emergence of Etruscan civilization is dated to the 8th–7th centuries BCE based on archaeological evidence, but the precise chronology is uncertain. The year 710 BCE is assigned speculatively as part of the broader development of Etruscan culture.

events.title: Possible dating of the Lelantine War between Euboean city-states (traditional dating)
events.description: The Lelantine War was a conflict between the Greek city-states of Chalcis and Eretria on the island of Euboea, fought over control of the fertile Lelantine Plain. The war is traditionally dated to the 8th century BCE, with some sources placing it around 710 BCE or earlier. However, modern scholarship is uncertain about the precise dating and even the historical reality of the war as a single, unified conflict. The war may have been a series of conflicts over several decades rather than a single event.

sources.name: Plutarch and other later Greek sources

events.certainty_note: The Lelantine War is attested in later Greek sources, but the precise dating is uncertain. Modern scholarship questions whether the war was a single, unified conflict or a series of conflicts over several decades. The traditional date of c. 710 BCE is speculative and may be significantly off.

disconfirming_evidence: The construction of Dur-Sharrukin is well-attested and confirmed by archaeology, so no disconfirming evidence is identified for that event. However, the Lelantine War's dating and even its historical reality as a single conflict are disputed by modern scholars. Some scholars argue that the war was a series of conflicts over several decades rather than a single event, and the traditional date of c. 710 BCE may be significantly off. The emergence of Etruscan civilization is also debated; some scholars argue that Etruscan culture developed gradually over the 8th–7th centuries BCE rather than emerging suddenly at a specific date.
historiographic_note: For 710 BCE, Assyrian sources remain the most reliable, with detailed inscriptions and archaeological evidence from Dur-Sharrukin providing contemporary documentation. Greek sources are sparse and often legendary; most surviving accounts were written centuries after the events. The bias toward Mesopotamian documentation means that events in Greece, Italy, and other regions are known primarily through much later sources or archaeological evidence. The dating of early Greek and Etruscan history is particularly uncertain due to the reliance on later sources and the imprecision of archaeological dating methods.

graph_edges.note: The construction of Dur-Sharrukin continued until Sargon II's death in 705 BCE
The dating and historical reality of the Lelantine War are disputed by modern scholars
